title: How often to apply sunscreen, symptoms of heat exhaustion and safety tips for Ireland’s hot spell - The Irish Times
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
Struggling with the hot weather this summer? While many people welcome the sunshine, it doesn’t come without risks. From sunburn to heat exhaustion, it’s important to take care during hot weather spells.
With that in mind, here are some tips for staying safe in the sun.
Wear sunscreen
Between 11am and 3pm are generally the hottest hours of the day. Try to avoid going out during this time.
If you do find yourself in the sun during these hours, make sure you continue to top up on the sunscreen. The HSE recommends you regularly apply a broad-spectrum water-resistant sunscreen. Broad spectrum means the sunscreen protects against ultraviolet (UV) rays, which can damage the skin. Adults should use an SPF (sun protection factor) of at least 30 and children should use SPF 50.
Sunscreen should be applied 20 minutes before going outside, and every two hours afterwards. Make sure to reapply after swimming or if you have sweated throughout the day.
Watch the UV index
Keep an eye on the UV index, which tells you how strong the sun’s UV rays are each day. UV rays can damage skin and increase your risk of skin cancers. Take extra precautions when the UV index is three or above.
Dress for the weather
Sunscreen alone does not give 100 per cent protection – so be sure to pack sunglasses that offer protection against UV rays and to have a broadbrimmed sun hat to protect both your head and face.
[ What to wear to stay cool during a heatwaveOpens in new window ]
Dr Suzanne Kelly, assistant medical director at the Irish College of General Practitioners, says parents should put children in “a wide coverage swimsuit” with “a hat covering the ears and the neck”.
Tan from a bottle
As much as you might want a sun-kissed glow, it really is best to get it from a bottle. A tan from the sun is a sign that your skin has been damaged. Such damage never goes away, even when it fades.
Keep your home cool
When inside, Kelly notes you should treat the room you’re in “like a fridge” during the day. This means keeping the room as cool as possible by keeping doors and windows closed, rather than letting the heat in.
[ Irish homes can feel stuffy in warmer weather. But there are simple ways to help it feel coolerOpens in new window ]
At night, when the temperature drops, keep windows open to stay cool while you sleep.
Stay hydrated
Staying hydrated is important in any weather, but especially during hot days. Rather than waiting until you’re thirsty to drink, sip water throughout the day. Most adults need about two litres of water every day to stay hydrated.
Kelly advises that consuming alcohol in high temperatures “is not a great idea”, saying it “can make you more dizzy and more dehydrated”.
Watch out for the symptoms of dehydration. These can include feeling lightheaded or dizzy, tiredness, a dry mouth, lips or tongue and urinating less throughout the day. Dehydrated babies may have fewer wet nappies than usual.
Don’t leave children in cars
Children do not sweat as much as adults, so it is harder for them to stay cool. Don’t leave children in parked cars, even if the window is open. Parked cars can heat up by at least 10 degrees in just 10 minutes.
Avoid heat exhaustion
Watch out for signs of heat exhaustion, such as body cramps, lack of appetite, headaches and vomiting. Heat exhaustion can turn to heatstroke if the body cannot cool down.
If you think your child may have heat exhaustion, sponge their skin with cool water and place cold packs around their armpits and neck.
Kelly notes that anyone who becomes nauseated, confused, suddenly doesn’t want to drink fluids or looks like they are developing a fever should receive medical attention.

title: Pasadena Health Officials Warn Residents to Spot Heat Illness as Temperatures Climb Into the 90s - Pasadena Now
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
Pasadena Health Officials Warn Residents to Spot Heat Illness as Temperatures Climb Into the 90s
The city's public health department identifies the symptoms that separate a trip to the shade from a trip to the emergency room
Published on Wednesday, July 8, 2026 | 6:03 am
A body temperature above 103 degrees, confusion, hot dry skin — those are the signs that something has gone dangerously wrong with a person out in the heat, and the Pasadena Public Health Department wants residents to know them before temperatures peak this week.
The department posted a social media advisory on Monday identifying the symptoms of heat exhaustion and heat stroke as a National Weather Service heat advisory took effect across the San Gabriel Valley. The advisory covers Pasadena, Altadena and surrounding areas from Tuesday, July 7 through Friday, July 10, with forecasters projecting highs of 90 to 92 degrees locally and triple-digit readings in some inland valleys.
The distinction between heat exhaustion and heat stroke matters.
Heat exhaustion — marked by muscle cramps, heavy sweating, nausea, vomiting, headache, paleness, a fast or weak pulse, and weakness or fainting — is the body’s warning that it is losing too much water and salt, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
Heat stroke is what happens when the warning goes unanswered. The sweating mechanism fails, body temperature spikes and the brain begins to shut down, the CDC says. It can cause permanent disability or death without emergency treatment.
The Pasadena health department’s infographic spells out the difference in plain terms: heat stroke brings dizziness, confusion, headache, unconsciousness, a rapid and strong pulse, very high body temperature, and red, hot, and dry skin. If someone shows those symptoms, call 911, the advisory states.
“Heat causes more deaths in the U.S. annually than floods, storms, and lightning combined,” said Muntu Davis, the Los Angeles County health officer, in the county’s press release accompanying the advisory. “Those most likely to get ill during the heat include older adults, young children, outdoor workers, athletes, and individuals with chronic medical conditions.”
Davis urged residents to act quickly if they see trouble. Anyone experiencing symptoms of heat stroke or heat exhaustion — dizziness, nausea, a rapid heartbeat, confusion or passing out — should seek medical help immediately, he said in the press release.
The NWS said the warmest temperatures during this stretch are expected Wednesday and Thursday afternoons, with only a very modest cooldown Friday. Temperatures are expected to remain above normal into the following week, according to the NWS.
Pasadena is one of only three cities in California that operates its own independent public health jurisdiction, giving its department direct authority to issue local advisories. The department, led by Director of Public Health Manuel Carmona, has been serving the community since 1892, according to the city’s website.
County health officials recommend drinking water throughout the day without waiting to feel thirsty, wearing lightweight and light-colored clothing with a hat or umbrella, wearing sunscreen with SPF 15 or higher, and planning outdoor activities to avoid the hottest hours of the day, according to the press release.
Residents without air conditioning can take cool showers or baths, reduce stove and oven use, and visit air-conditioned public spaces. The Jackie Robinson Community Center, at 1020 North Fair Oaks Avenue, has served as Pasadena’s designated cooling center during past heat events; residents can call (626) 744-7300 for current hours and availability. Pasadena’s public library branches are not official cooling centers but offer air-conditioned indoor relief during regular hours.
Cars heat up rapidly even with windows cracked, the county advisory warns. Residents should never leave children or pets unattended in vehicles and should call 911 if they see a child or pet alone in a car, the press release states.
Cooling centers, splash pads and community pools are available across Los Angeles County. Residents can find locations at ready.lacounty.gov/heat or by calling 211, which operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week, according to the county press release. For additional heat safety information, the Pasadena department’s advisory directed residents to Ready.gov/heat.
Pasadena Dial-A-Ride members can schedule transportation to cooling locations by calling (626) 791-7200. Pasadena Transit routes also serve community centers and libraries. For power emergencies during high heat, Pasadena Water and Power can be reached at (626) 744-4673.
Residents can sign up for the Pasadena Local Emergency Alert System at CityOfPasadena.net/Fire/PLEAS/ and visit the city’s Extreme Weather Portal at CityOfPasadena.net/extreme-weather-portal/ for weather forecasts, air quality data and emergency contacts. The City Service Center is available Monday through Friday during business hours at (626) 744-7311.
The heat advisory is the latest in a series issued for the region this year. Pasadena experienced an unusually intense March heat wave that pushed temperatures into the mid-90s — roughly 20 to 30 degrees above normal for the month — and prompted the city to open the Jackie Robinson Community Center as a cooling center.
The body’s early warnings are worth heeding. What starts as cramps and heavy sweat can turn into confusion and a medical emergency in the time it takes to finish a jog or a shift outdoors.

title: Mum slams 'fatal' eight-hour power outage on hottest day of year - Oxford Mail
search_term: ("mercury soars" OR "mercury crosses" OR "temperature soars" OR "record temperature" OR "all-time high" OR "degrees Celsius" OR "maximum temperature" OR "hottest day" OR "temperature crosses 40" OR "temperature crosses 45" OR "temperature likely to rise") Assam
full_text:
Charlotte, who did not wish for her surname to be published, has been a resident at the Elmsbrook housing development for five years.
Those living in the area were warned earlier this week by GTC that a power outage would take place from 8.30am to 4.30pm tomorrow (Thursday, July 9) for works.
According to the 36-year-old, this work has been scheduled for around three years, but a date was finally set this week.
READ MORE: Urgent public warning issued for Wednesday by UK firefighters
Thursday is set to be the hottest day of the year in Bicester (stock photo). (Image: Jordan Pettitt/PA Wire)
Charlotte and her neighbours have been left worried as this was set for the hottest day of the year for Bicester, with temperatures set to reach 34°C.
"The work has needed doing for years, as the issues relating to energy spikes during sunny periods cause damage to electrical goods," she said.
"However, if they could wait this long, it certainly should not have been planned last minute, during a well-forecasted significant heatwave.
"This is particularly the case due to the high number of vulnerable residents here, and I myself have a newborn baby, a toddler and an elderly dog at home.
"It coincides with the hottest day of the year for Bicester and will leave residents without power for fridges, freezers, fans and AC."
READ MORE: Cotswolds restaurant named among the very best in UK
The Elmsbrook housing development in the July heat. (Image: Public Submission)
The mother of two also said that the consequences could prove "fatal" due to the high number of vulnerable locals.
"There are also many vulnerable residents here, including the elderly, pets, and newborn babies, for whom being left without power in the predicted temperatures could be fatal," added Charlotte.
"Especially those with medical conditions requiring electronic medical equipment and refrigerated medication.
"Many residents, including those of the priority services register, had requested that the planned maintenance be moved to a day outside of the heat warning or for some assistance.
"They have been denied all support and simply told to go somewhere else, and this is not actually possible, feasible or safe."
READ MORE: Hollywood actress spotted drinking at Jeremy Clarkson's Cotswolds pub
With temperatures rising, concern has spread among residents (stock photo). (Image: Paul King)
The letter from GTC that was posted to residents in the area on Monday (July 6) said: "Why do we temporarily need to switch off your electricity?
"So we can provide you with a safe and reliable electricity supply, it is important that certain routine checks and maintenance works are carried out.
"We work closely with SSE, and they have explained they need to complete some of these checks. This means your electricity will be turned off at the above time.
"We understand this is not ideal and we are sorry for the inconvenience, but these works are vital to ensure a reliable supply of electricity is delivered to your property.
"Switching your electricity off for this period also ensures the safety of both the public and our engineers working on site. Thank you for your patience."
Residents then said earlier this afternoon (Wednesday, July 8), that the works had been postponed due to the heat.
READ MORE: Secret new Ali G movie wraps filming after 'shooting scenes' in Oxfordshire
The Elmsbrook housing development in the July heat. (Image: Public Submission)
Charlotte believes that this backtrack was caused by local political and press pressures on GTC.
"Several residents have consistently been told that despite the weather and concerns, the work would be going ahead.
"It was only after speaking to the Oxford Mail and MP Callum Miller that GTC suddenly decided to cancel. Clearly, just to avoid bad press.
"I just hope they are able to reschedule as soon as possible, during a period with typical weather to keep residents and their own staff who would be undertaking the work safe.
"This incident has caused a lot of residents a lot of stress, and I am sure many will be out of pocket as a result, trying to book somewhere cool to go."
READ MORE: Kate Moss 'embarks on new career' after falling in love in Cotswolds
Calum Miller, Liberal Democrat MP for Bicester. (Image: Contributed)
Liberal Democrat MP for Bicester, Calum Miller, said: "My team and I have been working hard to support residents by raising their concerns directly with GTC about the planned power outage in Elmsbrook during this heatwave.
"For many vulnerable residents, access to electricity is not a convenience - it can be essential for keeping cool, staying safe and powering vital medical equipment.
"I am pleased that GTC has listened to those concerns and cancelled the planned outage."
After being contacted for comment by this newspaper, a spokesperson for GTC siad: "GTC understands the concerns raised by residents regarding the planned power outage in Elmsbrook and, after carefully reviewing the forecast temperatures, we have taken the decision to postpone the planned interruption to electricity supplies.
"The works will now be rescheduled for a later date when the extreme heat has subsided.
"Customers will be notified of the postponement by text message, with further details of the rescheduled date provided in due course."

title: IMD issues red alert for three Kerala districts, warns of extremely heavy rain - Rediff
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
The IMD on Wednesday issued a red alert for three districts in Kerala, warning of extremely heavy rain, officials said.
The India meteorological department said a red alert had been issued for Malappuram, Kozhikode and Wayanad districts, indicating the likelihood of extremely heavy rainfall exceeding 20.4 cm.
An orange alert, indicating the possibility of very heavy rainfall between 11.5 cm and 20.4 cm, was issued for Idukki, Ernakulam, Thrissur, Palakkad, Kannur and Kasaragod districts for the day.
A yellow alert was issued for Pathanamthitta, Alappuzha and Kottayam, indicating heavy rainfall between 6.5 cm and 11.5 cm for the day.
For Thursday, the IMD has issued a yellow alert for Malappuram, Kozhikode, Wayanad, Kannur and Kasaragod districts.
The Kerala State Disaster Management Authority warned that the heavy rainfall could trigger landslides, mudslides, flash floods and waterlogging, particularly in hilly and low-lying areas. -- PTI

title: Five north Kerala districts on yellow alert on July 9 - The Times of India

full_text:
- Five north Kerala districts on yellow alert on July 9
Trending
Kochi/ Palakkad/ Idukki: Five northern districts, from Malappuram to Kasaragod, will be on yellow warning for heavy rainfall on Thursday. Meanwhile, the district collectors of Kozhikode, Wayanad, and Malappuram have declared a holiday for all educational institutions on Thursday. However, university examinations and PSC exams will be held as scheduled. Strong surface winds with speed occasionally reaching 50-60 kmph are likely to prevail over Kerala and Lakshadweep on Thursday.On Wednesday, except Kollam and Thiruvananthapuram, all other districts were either on yellow or orange alert. A significant amount of rainfall was also reported at many places on Wednesday, with Nilambur in Malappuram receiving 9cm rains from 8.30am to 5.30pm Urumi in Kozhikode recorded 7cm and Mannakkad in Palakkad received 5.5cm rains.While the state continues to experience a deficiency of 28% rainfall till Wednesday morning since June 1 this season. The state received only 59cm rains against a normal of 82cm during the period.Relief camp opened: A relief camp was opened in Palakkad after heavy rains prompted authorities to shift tribal families from Parachathi hill settlement to safer locations. The camp has been set up at Ayyappanpotta Cultural Centre in Malampuzha. A total of 13 people, including five men, three women and five children, are currently staying at the camp.Woman killed: A woman was killed after a tree branch fell on her while she was working in a cardamom estate at Mankavala near Chakkupallam in Kumily on Wednesday. The deceased was Perumayi, 61, a native of Gudalur in Tamil Nadu.

title: Raith Horata Samiti urges govt to implement drought relief measures - The Times of India
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
- Raith Horata Samiti urges govt to implement drought relief measures
Hubballi: The Karnataka Kalasa-Banduri Raith Horata Samiti has urged the govt to immediately implement relief measures for farmers affected by the prevailing drought conditions.Speaking to reporters here on Wednesday, Samiti president Siddanna Teji said protecting farmers and the public from hardship should be the govt’s top priority. He warned that if authorities failed to act promptly, farmers and people across the state would be compelled to launch a massive protest in the coming days.Teji demanded that the state govt announce a special drought-relief package and seek additional financial assistance from the Centre. He also called for an uninterrupted drinking water supply through tankers in rural areas. “Drought is not merely a farmers’ issue; it is a matter of the state’s food security, rural economy and the livelihoods of millions of people,” he said.He urged the govt to immediately conduct surveys in affected regions and declare drought-hit areas wherever necessary. Teji also demanded the immediate release of compensation to farmers on a per-acre basis, prompt disbursal of crop insurance claims, temporary suspension of loan recovery by agricultural and cooperative banks, and waiver of interest on farm loans.SB Patil, Renuka Hongal, Basheer M, Sujata S and others were present.
End of Article

title: Rain Brings Much-Needed Relief to Guwahati After Prolonged Heatwave - The Sentinel - of this Land, for its People
source: The Sentinel - of this Land, for its People
full_text:
Guwahati: Guwahati: After days of scorching temperatures and an unrelenting heatwave, today people of Guwahati finally experienced much-needed relief as several parts of the city received rainfall during the afternoon.
Today, Around 3 pm, moderate showers lashed areas including G.S raod, Six Mile, Rukminigaon, Downtown, Panjabari, Hatigaon and Beltola, bringing down temperatures and offering respite from the intense summer heat.
Meanwhile, other parts of the city, including Ulubari, Rehabari, Bharalumukh and Paltan Bazar, remained under dark, overcast skies, with rain-bearing clouds hovering over the areas, indicating a high probability of rainfall later in the day.
However, the brief afternoon shower was enough to cause artificial flooding in Maligaon, leaving several roads waterlogged and disrupting traffic.
As per weather observations, the city's temperature dropped to around 27°C following the rainfall, accompanied by cool breezes that provided a welcome change after days of oppressive weather conditions.
The sudden spell of rain transformed the atmosphere across the city, with overcast skies replacing the sweltering sunshine. The showers also brought temporary relief from the discomfort caused by the recent heatwave, although they led to minor traffic slowdowns in some areas during the afternoon rush hour.
The rainfall has raised hopes of more showers in the coming days, with many Guwahatians welcoming the change in weather after an extended spell of extreme heat.

title: Heavy Rain Causes Widespread Waterlogging Across Guwahati - The Sentinel - of this Land, for its People
source: The Sentinel - of this Land, for its People
full_text:
Guwahati: Today, after heavy rainfall caused widespread waterlogging across several parts of Guwahati, severely disrupting traffic movement.
Areas including Maligaon, Ambari, Rajgarh, Lamb Road, Kahilipara, Lokhra Road, Boragaon, Silpukhuri and Chandmari witnessed inundated roads after hours of rain. The National Highway beneath the Jorabat flyover was also submerged, bringing traffic on the lower stretch to a standstill.
In several areas , water levels reportedly rose up to knee height, leaving many vehicles stranded or breaking down. School buses and public transport vehicles were among those caught in the waterlogged roads, particularly in the Maligaon area, causing inconvenience to commuters and students.
Although traffic continued to move over the Jorabat flyover, congestion intensified as vehicles travelling in both directions were diverted to the elevated stretch due to flooding on the road below.
As per the India Meteorological Department (IMD), Guwahati is expected to witness generally cloudy skies with one or two spells of moderate rain on 8th July , after light rain and thunderstorms in the afternoon.
The IMD has further forecast generally cloudy conditions with intermittent rain or thundershowers across the city until 14th July, indicating that Guwahati is likely to experience wet weather over the coming week. Government have advised Guwahatians to remain cautious and plan their travel accordingly as further rainfall may aggravate waterlogging in low-lying areas.
